Paragraph.text: In recent years, the advancement of economic globalization and digitalization has accelerated the overseas expansion of Japanese companies, leading to increased profits from direct investment. According to balance of payments statistics, the surplus in the "primary income balance," which consists of dividends and other income received by Japanese companies from overseas subsidiaries, is projected to reach a record high of 41.7 trillion yen (preliminary figure) in fiscal year 2024. In order for Japan to achieve sustainable economic growth in the future, it is crucial to further strengthen the international competitiveness of its companies and capture overseas growth markets.
Institution: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry
Document.Title: Final Report of the Study Group on the International Tax System

Paragraph.text: On the other hand, looking at the environment surrounding overseas business activities, the international tax system is undergoing a period of dramatic change, as evidenced by the international agreement reached at the OECD/G20 in 2021 on new international tax rules, including "allocation of new taxing rights to market countries" and "global minimum taxation." In particular, global minimum taxation, which aims to curb the competition to lower corporate taxes by ensuring a tax of 15% or more on multinational corporations above a certain size, is being implemented in various countries.
Institution: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry

Paragraph.text: Against this backdrop, the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry convened the “Study Group on the Ideal Structure of the Minimum Tax Regime and the Controlled Foreign Company (CFC) Tax Regime” in 2022. The group examined how to reconcile Japan’s existing CFC rules, which were introduced to prevent international tax avoidance, with the global minimum tax, as well as ways to simplify the CFC regime. Meanwhile, since 2023, countries around the world have begun revising their CFC regimes in response to the introduction of the global minimum tax. In addition, Japan is set to begin applying the global minimum tax in fiscal year 2024, reflecting a changing environment. In light of these developments, there has been a growing need to reassess international taxation systems, including the CFC regime, to ensure they do not hinder the maintenance and enhancement of Japanese companies' international competitiveness. Accordingly, the “Study Group on the Ideal Structure of International Taxation Systems in Light of Japanese Companies’ Overseas Expansion Trends” was convened.
Institution: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry
Document.Title: Final Report of the Study Group on the International Tax System

Paragraph.text: This study group identified key issues within the CFC regime that could undermine the international competitiveness of Japanese firms. These include: the lack of clarity in the purpose of the system; excessive taxation on foreign subsidiaries that engage in genuine economic activities; and the substantial administrative burden arising from the coexistence of the global minimum tax and the CFC regime. Based on this assessment, the group examined potential directions for reforming the CFC system to address these challenges. It also reviewed the current state of foreign tax measures and their relationship with the global minimum tax.
Institution: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry

Paragraph.text: Japan is rapidly becoming an "Investment Nation," and profits that Japanese companies gain in growth markets overseas are very important to Japanese economy as population has been decreasing and thus the domestic market is shrinking. On the other hand, as the digital transformation of the economy accelerates, Japanese companies are facing increasing competition with foreign companies, and it has been pointed out that the challenges lie in price competitiveness and product development capabilities. In order to overcome these challenges, it is necessary to ensure a level playing field for Japanese and foreign companies and to support smooth business activities overseas from tax system side. For this reason—and taking into consideration that the GloBE rules that was internationally agreed upon in October 2021 has been discussed in the US, UK, EU, and elsewhere—METI will inaugurate the Study Group on the GloBE rules and Controlled Foreign Company Taxation in order to confirm points for future discussions toward the rule's smooth introduction in Japan, and to discuss how to organize its relation with and simplify "CFC taxation".
Institution: Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry

Paragraph.text: Our corporate tax system will need to be updated due to global tax developments relating to the Base Erosion and Profit Shifting initiative, or BEPS 2.0. Pillar 2 introduces, amongst other things, a global minimum effective tax rate of 15% for MNE groups with annual global revenues of 750 million euros or more, under its Global Anti-Base Erosion (or GloBE) Model Rules. What this means is that if such an MNE were to have an effective tax rate of less than 15% in Singapore at the group level, other jurisdictions such as its home jurisdiction can collect the difference up to 15%.
Institution: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Finance Lawrence Wong
Document.Title: Budget statement for 2022
text_clean: our corporate tax system will need to be updated due to global tax developments relating to the base erosion and profit shifting initiative, or beps 2.0. pillar 2 introduces, amongst other things, a global minimum effective tax rate of 15% for mne groups with annual global revenues of 750 million euros or more, under its global anti-base erosion (or globe) model rules. what this means is that if such an mne were to have an effective tax rate of less than 15% in singapore at the group level, other jurisdictions such as its home jurisdiction can collect the difference up to 15%.

Paragraph.text: We will adjust our tax system in response to Pillar 2 GloBE rules. We are exploring a top-up tax called the Minimum Effective Tax Rate, or “METR”. The METR will top up the MNE group’s effective tax rate in Singapore to 15%. IRAS will study this further and consult the industry on the design of METR. We will also continue to closely monitor international developments before making any decisions on the METR.
Institution: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Finance Lawrence Wong
Document.Title: Budget statement for 2022

Paragraph.text: At this stage, it is premature and difficult to determine the eventual fiscal impact of both pillars. As I mentioned just now, there will be a negative revenue impact under Pillar 1. METR might yield some additional tax revenue in the short term, but the eventual impact of Pillar 2 on our revenue will depend on how governments and companies respond. The net impact of both Pillars depends on the rules and details, which are still being developed by the Inclusive Framework on BEPS.
Institution: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Finance Lawrence Wong

Paragraph.text: While BEPS 2.0 may have reduced the scope for tax competition, it has not reduced global competition for investments. In fact, competition is likely to intensify as governments worldwide seek to restore and rebuild their economies after the effects of the pandemic. So there may be less tax competition but there will be other forms of competition. We will have to take this into consideration and ensure that Singapore remains one of the best places in the world for business.
Institution: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Finance Lawrence Wong

Paragraph.text: The recommendations in the final BEPS project report need to be implemented in many countries and regions including emerging and developing countries. Therefore, “Inclusive Framework on BEPS” has been established by OECD. As one of the latest subjects, the members have been discussing the Two-Pillar Solution to address the tax challenges arising from the digitalization of the economy (that is, establishing a taxing right for market jurisdictions [Amount A] and the simplified and streamlined application of the transfer pricing rules [Amount B] under Pillar One; and the global minimum tax under Pillar Two), and the content from the Amount B report released has been incorporated into the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines. Additionally, regarding Pillar Two, model rules and administrative guidance for introducing global minimum tax in each country were agreed and publicized. In Japan, Income Inclusion Rule (IIR) out of the global minimum tax was legislated in the FY2023 tax reform (it took effect in April 2024); and Undertaxed Profits Rule (UTPR) and Qualified Domestic Minimum Top-up Tax (QDMTT) were legislated in the FY2025 tax reform (they will take effect in April 2026).

Paragraph.text: Simultaneously, the assessment of Vietnam's policy and the acceleration of the development of domestic law applying a domestic minimum tax mechanism that meets standards to secure tax collection rights could begin as a pilot program in November 2023, expanding further in December 2023, and officially announcing the policy on January 1, 2024, when the global minimum tax rate takes effect. The domestic law will be developed based on comparisons with OECD regulations as well as topics on incentives and costs when applied
Institution: Ms. Nguyen Thy Nga, Director of the Institute for Policy and Development Strategy Management

Paragraph.text: Following the recent G7 agreement to endorse a global minimum tax rate of 15%, the Inclusive Framework (IF) on Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (or BEPS) has released a statement on a two-pillar solution to address the tax challenges arising from the digitalisation of the economy. One of the proposals is a global minimum tax rate of at least 15%. As at 1 July 2021, 130 countries and jurisdictions, including Singapore, have agreed to the statement. However, the detailed design elements and implementation plan for these new rules have yet to be finalised. Singapore will continue to be actively involved in these international discussions, and will engage the industry as the new rules take shape.
Institution: Senior Minister and Minister in charge of MAS

Paragraph.text: First, providing a conducive regulatory environment for businesses to grow remains key, especially so for new and innovative activities. A good example is the Payment Services Act, which provides for payment service providers to innovate and grow in Singapore while being regulated in a risk-proportionate manner. Another example is MAS’ FinTech regulatory sandbox which enables financial institutions and FinTech players to experiment with innovative financial products or services in a live environment but within a well-defined space and duration.
Institution: Mr Tharman Shanmugaratnam, Senior Minister and Minister in charge of Monetary Authority of Singapore

text_clean: for the 2023 tax reform, the nippon individual savings account (nisa) will be fundamentally expanded and made permanent to actively shift household assets from savings to investments and double people’s asset income. at the same time, we will take tax measures to strengthen the startup ecosystem fundamentally. in addition, to achieve a fairer and more neutral tax system, we will introduce measures to require a minimum burden on extremely high levels of income and a global minimum tax. in addition, we will establish a neutral tax system by selecting the timing of asset transfers.

Paragraph.text: The OECD/G20 "BEPS Inclusive Framework" discussion on international tax challenges arising from the digitalization of the economy resulted in an agreement on a two-pillar solution: Pillar 1 (allocation of new taxing rights to market countries) and Pillar 2 (global minimum taxation). The second pillar has been legislated in many countries, and in Japan, the corporate income tax system for global minimum taxation will be applied to fiscal years beginning on or after April 1, 2024.
Document.Title: BEPS 2.0 Dispute Resolution - The Need for Effective Dispute Prevention/Resolution in a Global Taxation Framework
text_clean: the oecd/g20 "beps inclusive framework" discussion on international tax challenges arising from the digitalization of the economy resulted in an agreement on a two-pillar solution: pillar 1 (allocation of new taxing rights to market countries) and pillar 2 (global minimum taxation). the second pillar has been legislated in many countries, and in japan, the corporate income tax system for global minimum taxation will be applied to fiscal years beginning on or after april 1, 2024.

Paragraph.text: However, it appears that the framework for dispute resolution corresponding to the new international taxation has not yet been sufficiently developed. In other words, Pillar 1: Multilateral Convention on amount A provides an innovative multilateral dispute prevention/resolution mechanism, but the prospects for its entry into force are uncertain, and no new dispute prevention/resolution measures for amount B are envisaged, while Pillar 2: global minimum taxation seems to not yet have been sufficiently developed in the event of disagreement on interpretation and application. It is expected that discussion on this point of view will progress before its implementation, considering the current efforts to improve mutual agreement procedure and the ICAP mechanism.

Paragraph.text: Under the current principles of international taxation, taxable income of each member of multinational enterprises (MNEs) is calculated on the assumption that individual companies are engaged in business transactions as a separate company dealing at an arm’s length (principle of separate accounting). However, MNEs can easily reduce their group tax burden by booking intangible assets that generate a significant amount of profits (assumed to be excess profits) on the books of group companies in tax haven countries granting low tax rates.
Institution: OKA Naoki, Tokyo Foundation for Policy Research, Certified Puclic Tax Accountant

Paragraph.text: This paper pays attention to the fact that a package of solutions worked out by the international community in January 2020 has boldly adopted the taxation of consolidated group income and a global minimum tax for income taxation of MNEs ,and explores the meaning of the package under international taxation law. Will the package be effective in restraining “ a race to the bottom” (tax rates reduction competition)?
Institution: OKA Naoki, Tokyo Foundation for Policy Research, Certified Puclic Tax Accountant

Paragraph.text: The recommendations in the final BEPS project report　need to be implemented in many countries and　regions including emerging and developing countries.　Therefore, “Inclusive Framework on BEPS” has been　established by OECD. As one of the latest subjects, the　members have been discussing the Two-Pillar　Solution to address the tax challenges arising from the　digitalization of the economy (that is, establishing a taxing right for market jurisdictions [Amount A] and the simplified and streamlined application of the transfer pricing rules [Amount B] under Pillar One; and the global minimum tax under Pillar Two), and the content from the Amount B report released has been incorporated into the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines. Additionally, regarding Pillar Two, model rules and administrative guidance for introducing global minimum tax in each country were agreed and publicized. In Japan, Income Inclusion Rule (IIR) out of the global minimum tax was legislated in the FY2023 tax reform (it took effect in April 2024); and Undertaxed Profits Rule (UTPR) and Qualified Domestic Minimum Top-up Tax (QDMTT) were legislated in the FY2025 tax reform (they will take effect in April 2026)

Paragraph.text: In Japan, based on these model rules, a corporate tax on the global minimum tax amount for each applicable fiscal year was established through the FY2023 tax reform, and revisions were also made in the FY2024 and FY2025 tax reforms from the perspective of clarifying the system based on the content of the implementation guidance and the content of international discussions.
Document.Title: Tax reform for fiscal Y2025, including partial amendments to corporate tax on the international minimum tax for each applicable fiscal year
text_clean: in japan, based on these model rules, a corporate tax on the global minimum tax amount for each applicable fiscal year was established through the fy2023 tax reform, and revisions were also made in the fy2024 and fy2025 tax reforms from the perspective of clarifying the system based on the content of the implementation guidance and the content of international discussions.

Paragraph.text: Corporate tax on the global minimum tax amount for each target fiscal year will be applied to target fiscal years beginning on or after April 1, 2024. It applies to multinational corporate groups whose total annual revenue worldwide is 750 million euros or more in two or more of the four target fiscal years immediately preceding the target fiscal year. The purpose is to ensure that income, excluding the real-base income exclusion, is taxed at a base rate of 15 or higher in each country. This system imposes a top-up tax on the parent company located in Japan until the tax burden (effective tax rate) in the two low-tax countries where the subsidiary is located reaches the base tax rate of 15.

Paragraph.text: Against this background, the partial revision of the Basic Circular on Corporate Tax has been carried out with full consideration of the intent of the Model Rules and related frameworks in interpreting the law. Furthermore, given that tax and accounting systems differ across countries, in cases where a uniform treatment cannot be established, only illustrative examples are provided. Accordingly, care must be taken to avoid interpretations that deviate from the intent of the legal provisions or the background of this system’s introduction, merely on the grounds that no example is given in the circular or that a specific matter is not explicitly provided for.

Paragraph.text: For Japanese MNE Groups, it is extremely important that tax certainty is achieved when incorporating the GloBE Rules (hereinafter "the Rules") into domestic legislation. In other words, to ensure the effectiveness and appropriateness of the Rules, we believe it is vital to introduce robust dispute prevention and resolution mechanisms that bind jurisdictions to prevent and eliminate double taxation.
Document.Title: Comments on the Public Consultation Document concerning Pillar Two - Tax Certainty for the GloBE Rules
text_clean: for japanese mne groups, it is extremely important that tax certainty is achieved when incorporating the globe rules (hereinafter "the rules") into domestic legislation. in other words, to ensure the effectiveness and appropriateness of the rules, we believe it is vital to introduce robust dispute prevention and resolution mechanisms that bind jurisdictions to prevent and eliminate double taxation.

Paragraph.text: To achieve this, the timing that an IIR, a UTPR, or a QDMTT begins to apply should be internationally harmonised considering the diverse fiscal years of MNE Groups in each jurisdiction. For example, in Japan, the IIR is expected to be enacted into domestic law by the end of March 2023 and to be effective for fiscal years beginning on or after April 1, 2024. If there are jurisdictions in which the application of the IIR and UTPR will begin after January 2024, we request that such jurisdictions not apply the IIR and UTPR to MNE Groups whose UPE is in Japan (particularly corporations with a fiscal year ending December).

Paragraph.text: To counter the erosion of the international tax base and the disruption of fair competition conditions among companies, stemming from the increase in businesses conducted by global corporations without physical bases in market countries and profit shifting to low-tax countries, we will steadily implement measures based on the "Agreement on Tax Challenges Associated with the Digitalization of the Economy" reached in October 2021 under the OECD/G20 "BEPS (Base Erosion and Profit Shifting) Inclusive Framework." Regarding "Pillar 1 (Allocation of New Taxing Rights to Market Countries)" of the agreement, we will promptly ratify the multilateral treaty after its conclusion and develop domestic laws while considering the increased burden on small and medium-sized enterprises. We will also proceed with the development of domestic laws under "Pillar 2 (Global Minimum Taxation)." Furthermore, we will formulate rules to increase transparency in cross-border capital flows.

Paragraph.text: Singapore is a member of the Inclusive Framework (“IF”) and was among more than 135 jurisdictions which joined a multilateral consensus reached on 8 October 2021 to reform international taxation rules and ensure that MNEs pay a fair share of tax wherever they operate. A two-pillar solution (commonly known as BEPS 2.0) under the consensus seeks to address the tax challenges arising from the digitalisation of the economy.
Document.Title: Global Anti-Base Erosion (GloBE) Rules and Domestic Top-up Tax (DTT)
text_clean: singapore is a member of the inclusive framework (“if”) and was among more than 135 jurisdictions which joined a multilateral consensus reached on 8 october 2021 to reform international taxation rules and ensure that mnes pay a fair share of tax wherever they operate. a two-pillar solution (commonly known as beps 2.0) under the consensus seeks to address the tax challenges arising from the digitalisation of the economy.

Paragraph.text: Singapore has implemented a DTT and the IIR under Pillar Two of the BEPS 2.0 initiative, which impose a minimum ETR of 15% on businesses’ profits from FYs starting on or after 1 January 20252. The MMT Act has been enacted to implement the IIR (which is referred to as the MTT in the MMT Act) and DTT. The MTT and DTT are based on the published GloBE Model Rules, Commentary3 and AGs4 (collectively referred to as the “GloBE rules”).

Paragraph.text: To ask the Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Finance what is the impact of the US proposal for a global minimum corporate tax rate on Singapore's tax regime, status as a hub for regional corporate headquarters and the economy.

Paragraph.text: Since 2019, the Inclusive Framework on Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS), comprising more than 130 members including the US and Singapore, has been in discussions to revise international corporate tax rules under the BEPS 2.0 Project. One of the proposals under discussion is the introduction of an internationally-agreed minimum effective corporate tax rate for large multinational enterprises (MNEs), whenever they operate.
Institution: Parliamentary Reply by Deputy Prime Minister, and Minister for Finance, Mr Heng Swee Keat

Paragraph.text: Although momentum towards an international consensus has accelerated, the negotiations and subsequent design of the new international rules on taxation of MNEs are not yet settled. It is therefore not possible to predict with a high degree of confidence what the impact on Singapore will be. For example, the potential net fiscal impact could range from significant downsides to some upside, depending on the final tax rules and how governments and businesses thereafter dynamically respond.

Paragraph.text: Since 2016, there have been concerted efforts to strengthen international co-operation in taxation matters, to stamp out harmful practices and to increase the taxes paid by multinational enterprises (MNEs).  These efforts were first initiated by the OECD to deal with the issue of Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (or BEPS) by MNEs. To ensure a wider international consensus, the discussions were subsequently broadened to include more than 130 jurisdictions through a platform called the Inclusive Framework (IF) on BEPS.
Institution: Parliamentary Reply by Minister for Finance, Mr Lawrence Wong:

Paragraph.text: The second proposal is the introduction of an internationally-agreed minimum corporate tax rate for large MNEs, wherever they operate. This is known as Pillar 2. The G7 and IF have proposed a minimum effective tax rate of at least 15% for MNE groups with global revenues above 750 million euros.
Institution: Parliamentary Reply by Minister for Finance, Mr Lawrence Wong:
Document.Title: Implications on Singapore from G7 Agreement on Global Minimum Corporate Tax Rate
text_clean: the second proposal is the introduction of an internationally-agreed minimum corporate tax rate for large mnes, wherever they operate. this is known as pillar 2. the g7 and if have proposed a minimum effective tax rate of at least 15% for mne groups with global revenues above 750 million euros.

Paragraph.text: Pillar 2 aims to put a floor on international corporate tax competition.  Essentially, under this proposal, MNEs will be subject to the minimum effective tax rate of 15% at the MNE group level, in every jurisdiction they operate.  Broadly speaking, this means that if a MNE group in Singapore is taxed at an effective rate of, say 10%, then its home jurisdiction will impose additional rules to require the group to pay an additional 5% in tax there.  Pillar 2 will therefore limit the effectiveness of tax incentives as a tool to encourage larger MNEs to invest in Singapore.
Institution: Parliamentary Reply by Minister for Finance, Mr Lawrence Wong:
Document.Title: Implications on Singapore from G7 Agreement on Global Minimum Corporate Tax Rate
text_clean: pillar 2 aims to put a floor on international corporate tax competition.  essentially, under this proposal, mnes will be subject to the minimum effective tax rate of 15% at the mne group level, in every jurisdiction they operate.  broadly speaking, this means that if a mne group in singapore is taxed at an effective rate of, say 10%, then its home jurisdiction will impose additional rules to require the group to pay an additional 5% in tax there.  pillar 2 will therefore limit the effectiveness of tax incentives as a tool to encourage larger mnes to invest in singapore.

Paragraph.text: Ms Mariam Jaafar asked how many MNEs in Singapore currently pay less than the global minimum corporate tax rate of 15%. The current proposal is to apply this minimum effective tax rate to MNEs that meet a certain revenue threshold, currently set at 750 million euros. There are about 1,800 such MNE groups in Singapore that would meet this criteria.

Paragraph.text: We expect that a majority of these MNE groups will have group effective tax rates below 15% in Singapore. Paragraph.text: Over the decades, Singapore has built a solid reputation and track record as an attractive place for business and substantial economic activity.  While a conducive tax environment has been helpful in attracting investments, it is not the decisive factor.  Having quality infrastructure, good connectivity, a skilled workforce, an open and business-friendly regime founded on the rule of law, are all far more important factors.  We must therefore double down on these competitive strengths.  In particular, the Government has been investing and will continue to invest in enterprise capabilities, including innovation and digitalisation, and in the skills of our workforce.

Paragraph.text: On Mr Kenneth Tiong‘s question on grants, all incentives offered by our economic agencies, including the Refundable Investment Credit, are subject to terms and conditions. Companies that receive Government support must make significant investments in Singapore and meet key outcomes, such as job creation and local employment, business spending, or fixed asset investment. The level of support and conditions vary depending on the nature of the project and incentive. Should a company fail to meet the imposed conditions, the Government has mechanisms to terminate the incentive and claw back the grants provided.

Paragraph.text: According to the United States’ presidential memorandum on the OECD Global Tax Deal dated 20 January 2025, the United States Treasury has 60 days to deliver its recommendations on actions against non-compliance with any tax treaty with the United States, or tax rules that are extraterritorial or disproportionately affect American companies. At this stage, it is unclear what measures the United States will take, and how other jurisdictions will respond. We have prepared for different possible scenarios and will continue to monitor the issue as it develops.

Paragraph.text: As a responsible international tax jurisdiction, Singapore works with global partners such as the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)/G20 Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) Project to address tax avoidance by multinational enterprises.

Paragraph.text: a) More than 50 jurisdictions are taking steps towards implementing the global minimum tax framework under Pillar 2, also known as the Global Anti-Base Erosion (GloBE) rules. Some, like the European Union and United Kingdom, are working towards implementing the GloBE rules in phases from 2024, with full effects in 2025. Others, like Hong Kong and Thailand, are working towards implementing the rules from 2025.

Paragraph.text: BEPS 2.0 and other global developments have changed the nature of competition for investments around the world. With BEPS 2.0 setting a global minimum effective tax rate of 15% for large multi-national enterprises (MNEs), there will be less scope to use tax incentives as a tool to attract new investments going forward. At the same time, amidst geopolitical tensions and increased global competition for quality investments, countries are intensifying their efforts to attract new projects by providing generous subsidies and tax credits, particularly in strategic sectors such as semiconductors and clean energy:

Paragraph.text: Like other countries, Singapore will have to deal with these new competitive dynamics. The good news is that we are moving forward from a position of strength. Tax factors have never been the main reason why businesses choose to operate in Singapore. Investors are drawn to our stable and trusted business environment, well-trained workforce, and excellent connectivity, among other factors. But these did not come about by chance; they are the results of decades of investment in our infrastructure, our ecosystem, and our people.

Paragraph.text: Given the more challenging external environment, we must redouble our efforts to remain relevant and competitive and we can do so in three ways.
Institution: Ms Indranee Rajah, Minister in the Prime Minister’s Office, Second Minister for Finance and National Development
Document.Title: Opening Address by Ms Indranee Rajah, Minister in the Prime Minister’s Office, Second Minister for Finance and National Development, at Tax Academy Conference 2023 on 29 September 2026
text_clean: given the more challenging external environment, we must redouble our efforts to remain relevant and competitive and we can do so in three ways.

Paragraph.text: First, we will continue to align our regime with internationally agreed standards. Singapore has always supported a multilateral consensus-based approach that promotes global cooperation and creates a level playing field for businesses and jurisdictions alike. To this end, we have over the years aligned our tax regime with international standards in areas such as country-by-country reporting and tax transparency through exchange of information. We will continue this approach, to provide certainty to businesses that their practices in Singapore will be consistent with international standards. This will simplify their cross-border compliance and reporting burden.
Institution: Ms Indranee Rajah, Minister in the Prime Minister’s Office, Second Minister for Finance and National Development

Paragraph.text: Second, we will ensure continued ease of doing businesses for enterprises in Singapore. As we implement the GloBE rules, we need to take into account the compliance burden on businesses. The rules are new and complex, and we are mindful of businesses’ concerns regarding onerous compliance requirements and possible double taxation. Having clarity and consistency in implementing the rules globally will therefore be important.
Institution: Ms Indranee Rajah, Minister in the Prime Minister’s Office, Second Minister for Finance and National Development

Paragraph.text: We had earlier announced plans to implement the GloBE rules and a domestic top-up tax from businesses’ financial years starting on or after 1 January 2025. We will continue to monitor international developments and make adjustments along the way if needed. In designing our rules, we will take close reference to the GloBE Model Rules and Administrative Guidance, so that our domestic top-up tax can qualify for the Qualified Domestic Minimum Top-up Tax safe harbour. This will benefit MNEs which are based here. At the same time, we will continue to strive to make compliance easy and seamless for businesses as far as possible, not just in relation to BEPS 2.0 rules, but across all tax types.

Paragraph.text: On December 20, the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) publicly released the "Addressing the Tax Challenges of the Digital Economy – A Legislative Template for the Global Anti-Base Erosion Rule in Pillar Two" (hereinafter referred to as the "Pillar Two Legislative Template") on its official website, marking the basic completion of the Pillar Two scheme design. The Pillar Two Legislative Template will serve as a crucial foundation for national legislation to implement the global anti-base erosion rule. It clarifies the specific design of relevant rules regarding the minimum tax level to be paid by large multinational corporations on income generated from their operations in each jurisdiction worldwide. By establishing a global minimum tax system, it aims to combat tax evasion by multinational corporations and set a baseline for competition in corporate income tax rates. The global minimum tax rate for Pillar Two is 15%, which is expected to increase global corporate income tax revenue by $150 billion annually.

Paragraph.text: First, measures should be taken to strengthen macro policy coordination in response to such global challenges as inflation, food and energy insecurity, and prevent serious negative spillovers from policy adjustments of certain countries. Second, efforts are needed to mobilize development finance at a broad scale and undertake objective analytical work on the root causes of debt vulnerability. Multilateral creditors, such as the World Bank, and commercial creditors should also take steps to implement debt treatments in accordance with the principle of "joint action and equitable burden-sharing". Third, efforts should also be made to facilitate investment in infrastructure and to give full play to the G20's role in building consensus and mobilizing resources, so as to provide more high-quality, sustainable infrastructure investment that will deliver benefits to the people. Fourth, following the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities, G20 countries should promote international cooperation in response to climate change as they pursue development. Fifth, they should work together to build a fair and sustainable modern international taxation system, taking into account country circumstances and concerns, to advance the relevant work of the Two-Pillar Solution to Address Tax Challenges Arising from the Digitalisation of the Economy.

Paragraph.text: Against the backdrop of economic globalization, the issue of Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) has attracted significant attention from global political leaders, media, and the public. In September 2013, the BEPS Action Plan, an international tax reform project commissioned by the G20 and undertaken by the OECD, received endorsement from world leaders at the G20 St. Petersburg Summit, officially launching the BEPS Action Plan. In October 2015, the final results of the 15 BEPS Action Plans were released, and full implementation began on January 1, 2016, officially ushering in the BEPS 1.0 era for international tax governance. In January 2019, the BEPS Inclusive Framework further proposed a "two-pillar" international tax reform plan, announcing the start of the BEPS 2.0 era.
Document.Title: Characteristics and Achievements of BEPS International Tax Reform

Paragraph.text: The BEPS Action Plan has been in place for a full decade, and the topic of international tax reform has risen to an unprecedented level on the global political agenda. In terms of its development, the BEPS Action Plan has achieved five remarkable accomplishments and exhibits six recurring characteristics.

Paragraph.text: Between 2016 and 2018, the OECD published the "International VAT Guide," "Mechanisms for Effective Collection of VAT/GST by Suppliers in Non-Taxable Jurisdictions," and "The Role of Digital Platforms in the Collection of VAT/GST on Online Sales," providing policy guidance to various jurisdictions. According to OECD statistics, 50 jurisdictions have now established rules for levying VAT on B2C services and intangible assets sold online by foreign suppliers, with 40 of these jurisdictions implementing simplified registration and reverse collection systems.

Paragraph.text: With the widespread rise of the sharing economy and gig economy worldwide, the OECD published three guidelines between 2019 and 2021: "The Sharing and Gig Economy: Effective Tax Administration of Platform Sellers," "Model Rules for Platform Operators' Reporting of Information on Relevant Sellers in the Sharing and Gig Economy," and "The Impact of the Growth of the Sharing and Gig Economy on the Design and Administration of Value Added Tax Policy." These guidelines provide optional tax policy "prescriptions" for the healthy and standardized development of the platform economy. According to the OECD, 25 jurisdictions have already signed the Multilateral Competent Authority Agreement, which allows for the automatic exchange of information collected by digital platform operators regarding transactions and revenues realized by platform sellers in the sharing and gig economy and through the sale of goods via such platforms.

Paragraph.text: In March 2018, the OECD released "The Tax Challenges of Digitalization: Mid-Year Report 2018," marking a watershed moment in the upgrade of the Business of Tax Reform (BEPS) Action Plan. The report pointed out that digitalization did not create unique BEPS problems, but highly digitalized business models, characterized by non-physical cross-border operations and reliance on the synergy of intangible assets (including intellectual property), data, users, and intellectual property (IP), exacerbated these problems. Consequently, the BEPS Action Plan moved towards its 2.0 era. After going through stages such as "policy explanation and unified approach" (2019), "reform blueprint" (2020), "political consensus" (2021), and "model rules" (2021-2022), along with a series of rolling consultations, the "two-pillar" international tax reform plan has now been largely finalized and has officially entered the crucial stage of implementation.

Paragraph.text: Among them, the multilateral convention on the Amount Rule (Pillar 1) will be open for signing in the second half of this year, with a signing ceremony expected before the end of the year; the final text of the Amount Rule (Pillar 2) will be completed before the end of this year, and will be implemented after being approved by the BEPS Inclusive Framework in January 2024, and incorporated into the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines. The Global Base Erosion (GLoBE) rule (Pillar 2) is in a steady implementation phase, with more than 50 jurisdictions actively advancing or completing domestic legislation. The Model Taxable Rules (STTR) were also published on July 17 this year, and its multilateral implementation tool will be published and open for signing on October 2 this year.

Paragraph.text: —Eliminating the Impact of Mixed Mismatch Arrangements (Item 2) The report recommends that jurisdictions clean up any differential tax treatment arrangements existing within their jurisdictions to prevent taxpayers from deducting the same expense multiple times in different jurisdictions, or from deducting expenses when there is no corresponding taxable income. In 2017, the OECD again released a report on eliminating the impact of branch mismatch arrangements, providing guidance for jurisdictions to address similar mismatch issues arising from branch structures. According to the International Tax Research Platform (IBFD), 41 jurisdictions, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Australia, and New Zealand, have adopted the recommendations of this report.

Paragraph.text: The Controlled Foreign Company (CFC) Rule (Section 3) report, presented in a modular format, offers recommendations for designing effective CFC rules. These recommendations primarily cover the definition of CFCs, exemption rules and applicable thresholds, eliminating double taxation risks, and the definition, calculation, and classification of income. According to IBFD statistics, 58 jurisdictions have already adopted the CFC reporting recommendations.

Paragraph.text: —The recommendations in the report on limiting interest deductions (item 4) aim to prevent multinational corporations from using interest expenses to achieve excessive interest deductions, tax exemptions, or tax deferrals, thereby engaging in profit shifting. According to IBFD statistics, 69 jurisdictions have already introduced interest restriction rules. Among them, EU member states have fully implemented the EU Anti-Tax Avoidance Directive, limiting taxpayers' deductible borrowing costs to 30% of their EBITDA.

Paragraph.text: — Combating harmful tax practices (item 5, the minimum standard) is peer-reviewed and promoted by the Forum on Harmful Tax Practices (FHTP). According to the OECD, since the peer review mechanism was launched in 2016, the FHTP has reviewed 319 preferential regimes, promoted substantive legislation in 12 jurisdictions with no tax or only nominal taxation, coordinated the development of tax ruling information exchange frameworks for 131 jurisdictions, and facilitated nearly 50,000 exchanges of tax ruling information among governments.

Paragraph.text: Third, it reshaped the international rules on tax treaties and transfer pricing. Ultimately, it was incorporated into Action Plans 6-10 of the BEPS (Business and Economic Forum on Tax Treaties) in the Model Tax Treaties and Transfer Pricing Guidelines published by the OECD and the United Nations, thus reshaping the international rules on tax treaties and transfer pricing.

Paragraph.text: From the perspective of tax treaties, the main purpose test (PPT), interest restriction clauses (LOB), and anti-cause-of-conduit rules recommended to prevent tax treaty abuse (Item 6, the minimum standard) are mainly implemented through the Multilateral Convention on the Implementation of Tax Treaty-Related Measures to Prevent BEPS (BEPS MLI) or bilateral tax treaty negotiations. The latest peer review report (5th) of the OECD on the implementation of the minimum standard of Action Plan 6, published on March 21, 2023, shows that as of May 31, 2022, more than 1,050 tax treaties concluded by 118 Inclusive Framework member jurisdictions met this standard. Meanwhile, the proposed adjustments to the definition of a permanent establishment (PE) and its determination rules to prevent artificial circumvention of PE (Item 7) have been incorporated into Chapter 4 of the BEPS MLI and are planned to be implemented through bilateral tax treaties. To date, about half of the 100 signatories of the Multilateral Convention (MLI) have adopted MLI provisions implementing PE changes, but some countries have raised reservations about the rule; for example, the Netherlands raised reservations about provisions for circumventing permanent establishments through contractual splitting.

Paragraph.text: From a transfer pricing perspective, the development of transfer pricing guidelines (items 8-10) for intangible assets, risk and capital, and other high-risk transactions focuses on ensuring that transfer pricing outcomes are commensurate with value creation. The institutional transformation results show that action plan recommendations 8-10 have been incorporated into Chapter 1, Section 4 (Application of the Arm's-Length Principle), Chapter 2 (Transfer Pricing Methodology), Chapter 6 (Intangible Assets), Chapter 7 (Intra-Group Services), and Chapter 8 (Cost Sharing Agreements) of the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines. In recent years, the OECD has also successively published the Revised Guidance on the Profits Split Approach to Transactions (2018), the Additional Guidance on the Application of Tax Administration to Intangible Assets That Are Difficult to Value (2018), and the new version of the Transfer Pricing Guidelines for Financial Transactions (2020). These rules were all incorporated into the 2022 version of the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines on January 20, 2022.

Paragraph.text: First, "Measuring and Monitoring BEPS" (Item 11) establishes a quantitative methodology for collecting and analyzing the impact of tax avoidance on the economic and fiscal health of jurisdictions, as well as the impact of proposed measures under the BEPS Action Plan on jurisdictions. Due to limitations in accessing high-quality data, the BEPS Inclusive Framework only launched its corporate tax statistics database in January 2019. On November 17, 2022, the OECD published "Corporate Tax Revenue Statistics (4th Edition)," which includes the latest data from nearly 7,000 multinational companies' country-by-country reports (CbCRs) across 160 tax jurisdictions. This includes corporate income tax (CIT) revenue, statutory CIT rates, effective CIT rates, R&D tax incentives, implementation status of BEPS Action Plan 13, CbCR statistics, IP incentive schemes, and withholding tax standard rates. It also emphasizes the persistent risks of cross-border tax avoidance and the necessity of rapidly implementing the "two pillars" approach.

Paragraph.text: Secondly, the mandatory disclosure rule (item 12) provides a suggested framework for the disclosure of transaction details, methods of disclosure, related penalties, and information usage. According to IBFD statistics, 42 jurisdictions, including Canada, South Africa, Israel, and South Korea, have already implemented mandatory disclosure rules. Meanwhile, the EU is seeking public comment on the 8th version of the Directive on Cooperation in Tax Administration (DAC8), which extends the mandatory disclosure and exchange of tax information mechanisms among EU member states to include information on crypto-asset transaction income and pre-determination tax rulings for the wealthiest (high-net-worth) individuals. According to the OECD, as of the end of 2022, information on over 123 million financial accounts worldwide was automatically exchanged between jurisdictions, involving total assets exceeding €12 trillion. In June 2023, the OECD Ministerial Council recommended promoting the Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) and the revised Common Reporting Standard (CRS) published in October 2022 as international standards, aiming to further expand the scope of CRS to crypto-asset.

Since the first exchange in June 2018, as of September this year, more than 110 jurisdictions worldwide have established domestic legal frameworks for CbCRs, and 88 jurisdictions have signed multilateral or bilateral competent authority agreements allowing the exchange of CbCRs for multinational corporations.

Paragraph.text: Finally, the Dispute Resolution (Item 14, the minimum standard) Action Plan makes recommendations on resolving cross-border tax disputes to better implement the Mutual Agreement Procedure (MAP) provisions. Data released by the OECD shows that MAP cases in 127 jurisdictions generally have positive outcomes. In 2021, approximately 75% of transfer pricing and other types of MAP cases were resolved satisfactorily, but settlement still takes a long time. Transfer pricing cases averaged 32 months (compared to 35 months in 2020), while other types of cases averaged 21 months (compared to 18.5 months in 2020).

Paragraph.text: Fifth, it has improved the efficiency of amending bilateral tax treaties. The BEPS MLI (Item 15) Action Plan allows governments to promptly translate the outcomes of the BEPS Action Plan into bilateral tax treaty provisions to address relevant gaps. In November 2016, more than 100 jurisdictions concluded BEPS MLI negotiations. On June 7, 2017, the OECD held its first collective signing ceremony for the BEPS MLI, with 67 countries and regions, including my country, signing the convention, which entered into force on July 1, 2018. According to the OECD, as of September 7, 2023, a total of 100 jurisdictions have signed the BEPS MLI, of which 83 have ratified, accepted, or approved the convention.

Paragraph.text: First, the international multilateral governance framework for taxation is moving from superficial globalization to substantive co-governance. Over the past decade, the number of member countries participating in the BEPS Action Plan has expanded from the initial 61 to 143. Moreover, a new international multilateral governance framework for taxation is taking shape, evident in its organizational structure, substantive institutions, procedural rules, and the multilateral consensus reached. Among these, the CRS and CbCR, implemented to prevent harmful tax competition, represent procedural cooperation, have limited impact on national tax authority, and have progressed rapidly with significant results. In contrast, the "two-pillar" approach, which involves the transfer of national fiscal and economic sovereignty, has progressed more slowly.
Document.Title: Characteristics and Achievements of BEPS International Tax Reform
text_clean: first, the international multilateral governance framework for taxation is moving from superficial globalization to substantive co-governance. over the past decade, the number of member countries participating in the beps action plan has expanded from the initial 61 to 143. moreover, a new international multilateral governance framework for taxation is taking shape, evident in its organizational structure, substantive institutions, procedural rules, and the multilateral consensus reached. among these, the crs and cbcr, implemented to prevent harmful tax competition, represent procedural cooperation, have limited impact on national tax authority, and have progressed rapidly with significant results. in contrast, the "two-pillar" approach, which involves the transfer of national fiscal and economic sovereignty, has progressed more slowly.

Paragraph.text: Second, the hardening of "soft law" in the international tax arena is becoming increasingly apparent. For a long time, various manuals, guidelines, templates, annotations, and policy recommendations provided by international organizations to countries have been technical guidance texts, possessing only a certain degree of "soft law" nature and lacking mandatory and binding force. However, starting with the four minimum standards of BEPS 1.0, supplemented by peer review supervision, international commitments have had a direct impact on the transformation of domestic law. In the BEPS 2.0 stage, the "Pillar One" multilateral convention established partial taxation rights and dispute resolution mechanisms, hailed by the industry as the "first international public tax law"; the "Pillar Two" setting of the "if you don't tax, I will" coupling logic will ultimately force jurisdictions with actual tax rates below 15% to introduce "Pillar Two" rules to protect their own tax interests.

Paragraph.text: Third, the century-old international tax rules system is being gradually reformed and reshaped. In the BEPS 1.0 era, apart from BEPS Action Plan 1, the remaining action plans were mainly designed and implemented around the value creation principle (taxation of profits in the place where economic activity occurs and where value is created). In the BEPS 2.0 era, the value creation principle is almost never mentioned, and there is a lack of clear logical argumentation on how the benefit principle and economic loyalty principle apply to globalization and digitalization. Moreover, "Pillar Two" shifts the focus of international tax reform from harmful tax competition and tax information exchange to the double non-taxation problem caused by unfair competition taxation and tax evasion activities, forming a new interpretation of the single tax principle. In practice, the century-old permanent establishment (PE) principle, arm's length principle (ALP), and separate accounting principle (SAP) are being broken by the new calculation method of "Pillar One" A.

Paragraph.text: Fourth, various anti-tax avoidance rules are becoming increasingly stringent. Both developing and developed countries are actively drawing on the BEPS Action Plan to implement various localized anti-tax avoidance policies. For example, although India has not adopted the thin capitalization rule, it requires that, since April 2018, companies or private equity firms with interest expenses exceeding 10 million rupees (approximately 880,000 RMB) on non-resident related enterprises can only deduct up to 30% of their EBITDA (item 4); South Africa's 2022 Tax Law Amendment clarifies the rules for recognizing royalties obtained by CFCs (item 3); and the EU has incorporated mismatch (item 2), CFCs, and the restricted interest deduction rule into the EU Anti-Tax Avoidance Directive, requiring member states to implement them through domestic legislation.
Document.Title: Characteristics and Achievements of BEPS International Tax Reform

Paragraph.text: Fifth, tax transparency standards have seen a leap forward in a short period. From the review of harmful tax systems under BEPS Action 5, to the requirement in November 2018 that low (zero) tax jurisdictions (i.e., tax havens) establish corresponding economic substance standards in the BEPS Inclusive Framework, and then to the replacement of previous measures against harmful tax competition with restrictions on tax competition in Pillar 2, all these implicitly reflect the increased demands of the BEPS project for tax transparency of multinational corporations. Simultaneously, many countries are promoting further disclosure of certain types of tax planning activities (Action 12), introducing collaborative reporting obligations on tax-related information through digital platforms (Action 1), and promoting the implementation of a registration system for beneficial owners of high-net-worth individuals. The latest OECD reports, "Enhancing International Tax Transparency of Real Estate" and "Promoting the Exchange of Tax Treaty Information for Non-Tax Purposes," also demonstrate this trend of increasing tax transparency standards.

Paragraph.text: Sixth, multilateral cooperation mechanisms that accompany the BEPS Action Plan have laid the foundation for future tax reforms. Over the past decade, various multilateral cooperation mechanisms have moved towards broader and higher levels. For example, the OECD has collaborated with the African Forum on Tax Administration, the Inter-American Centre for Tax Administration, the European Tax Administration Organization, the Asian Organization for Tax Administration and Research, and tax cooperation platforms to develop tax governance systems. Furthermore, organizations such as the International Monetary Fund, the United Nations, the World Bank, and the Group of 77 have been deeply involved in international tax issues. In particular, projects such as the International Tax Compliance Assurance Programme (ICAP), joint audits, the Joint International Working Group on Intelligence Sharing and Collaboration (JITSIC), the Tax Technology Initiative List (ITTI), and the Digital Tax Auditor Without Borders (TIWB-DTA) have provided opportunities for the implementation and expansion of the BEPS Action Plan. They have also provided a foundation for policy design and tax administration for scenarios such as the gig economy, telemedicine, AI services, and the metaverse, as well as for the coordination and formulation of tax systems other than corporate income tax.

Paragraph.text: The Business of Taxation (BEPS) Action Plan has been in place for ten years, during which international tax governance has entered a new phase, and its positive results are evident. However, to ensure that all jurisdictions can uniformly understand and apply the rules, conservative estimates suggest that the English text of the OECD's "two-pillar" rules has exceeded 2,000 pages, highlighting its complexity. Therefore, facing questions such as the impact of the highly detailed "two-pillar" rules on national tax laws and regulations, whether a court's ruling on a "two-pillar" tax dispute will affect enforcement standards in other jurisdictions, and how taxpayers and tax authorities should respond to the "two-pillar" "top-down" tax management model, all tax jurisdictions worldwide should actively and thoroughly explore solutions to achieve a balance between fair and reasonable international tax governance and protecting their own tax sovereignty.

Paragraph.text: We will adopt the same approach for BEPS 2.0. The Inclusive Framework is still working through the parameters. Paragraph.text: The Revenue Department, through the Ministry of Finance, proposed the draft Emergency Decree on Top-up Tax to the Cabinet, which approved it on 11th December 2024. On 26th December 2024, the Emergency Decree on Top-up Tax, B.E. 2567 (2024), was officially promulgated in the Royal Gazette. The Emergency Decree will apply to large multinational enterprises (MNEs) with consolidated financial statement revenues of at least €750 million and will take effect for accounting periods commencing on or after 1st January 2025
Document.Title: The Royal Decree on Additional Tax
text_clean: the revenue department, through the ministry of finance, proposed the draft emergency decree on top-up tax to the cabinet, which approved it on 11th december 2024. on 26th december 2024, the emergency decree on top-up tax, b.e. 2567 (2024), was officially promulgated in the royal gazette. the emergency decree will apply to large multinational enterprises (mnes) with consolidated financial statement revenues of at least €750 million and will take effect for accounting periods commencing on or after 1st january 2025

Paragraph.text: The enactment of the Emergency Decree on Top-up Tax aims to enable Thailand to retain its right to collect additional tax revenue, thereby safeguarding the nation’s interests. Without such legislation, Thailand risks losing potential tax revenue to jurisdictions that have already implemented comparable Top-up Tax laws. By 2024, 28 countries have enacted such laws, including Greece, South Korea, Canada, Japan, Denmark, Turkey, the Netherlands, New Zealand, France, Finland, Germany, Spain, Sweden, the United Kingdom, Australia, Italy, Ireland, and Vietnam. For  2025, countries such as Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and Hong Kong are expected to follow suit. The Emergency Decree on Top-up Tax applies to large MNEs operating in Thailand, whether they are Thai MNEs investing abroad or foreign MNEs investing in Thailand. These MNEs must have annual revenues of at least €750 million in the consolidated financial statements of their Ultimate Parent Entity (UPE) for at least two of the four accounting periods immediately preceding the accounting period under consideration. The Top-up Tax will be levied at the globally agreed minimum tax rate, in alignment with the Global Minimum Tax framework, thereby establishing a floor on tax competition. The Revenue Department drafted the Emergency Decree in accordance with the standards established by the OECD, ensuring consistency with similar laws in other jurisdictions, thereby reducing the compliance burden for MNEs investing in Thailand.

Paragraph.text: Pinsai Suraswadi, Director-General of the Revenue Department, states that the enactment of the Emergency Decree represents a significant milestone in promoting investment while simultaneously ensuring Thailand’s fiscal sustainability. Following the Emergency Decree’s enactment, the Revenue Department will propose secondary legislation to provide additional details in accordance with the standards established by the OECD. In respect of tax filings, tax payments, the submission of GloBE Information Returns, and notifications, the Revenue Department will ensure these processes are seamlessly facilitated through a fully electronic system. To support compliance with the law, the Revenue Department will host seminars and develop e-learning platforms to enhance awareness and understanding among taxpayers and key stakeholders, including tax advisors and auditors. Taxpayers and stakeholders are encouraged to stay updated through the Revenue Department’s official website and social media channels.

Paragraph.text: The justification and necessity for the restriction of rights and liberties of individuals under this Emergency Decree are to enable Thailand to comply with obligations and commitments on the exchange of tax information and financial account information in accordance with international agreements on taxation and to ensure tax collection is fair and efficient, which will benefit the country's economy. In this regard, the enactment of this Emergency Decree duly complies with the conditions provided in section 26 of the Constitution of the Kingdom of Thailand
Document.Title: Emergency Decree on the Exchange of Information under International Agreement on Taxation
text_clean: the justification and necessity for the restriction of rights and liberties of individuals under this emergency decree are to enable thailand to comply with obligations and commitments on the exchange of tax information and financial account information in accordance with international agreements on taxation and to ensure tax collection is fair and efficient, which will benefit the country's economy. in this regard, the enactment of this emergency decree duly complies with the conditions provided in section 26 of the constitution of the kingdom of thailand

Paragraph.text: The reason for the promulgation of this Emergency Decree is that Thailand is obliged to comply with International Agreements on the Exchange of Tax Information on Request According to the Agreements or Conventions on the Avoidance of Double Taxation and the Prevention of Tax Evasion and the Multilateral Convention on Mutual Administrative Assistance in Tax Matters and has to undertake its obligations on automatic exchange of financial account information according to the Multilateral Competent Authority Agreement on the Automatic Exchange of Financial Account Information and that Thailand is a member of the Global Forum on Transparency and Exchange of Information for Tax Purposes, which is a cooperation framework for the exchange of tax information of the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), and therefore, Thailand has to proceed to exchange tax information on request and automatically exchange financial account information with the members of such agreements within September 2566 B.E. (2023). These actions are new and operationally complex, requiring reporting financial institutions, to be provided with time to examine and prepare financial account information in their possession and supervision for exchange purposes. If financial institutions in Thailand are unable to exchange information within the specified timeframe, Thailand will be classified as anon-cooperative jurisdiction for tax purposes by the European Union and OECD group of countries, Thailand's key trading partners. This could significantly and unavoidably impact economic security of the country. Therefore, the government submitted a bill on such a matter to the House of Representatives, and the committee members finished the consideration. However, as a session of the House of Representatives has earlier been prorogued, such a bill is pending consideration by the House of Representatives. If an Emergency Decree is not enacted, there will be no legal framework requiring compliance with those obligations. This situation constitutes an emergency that necessitates urgent action to preserve the country's economic security. Thus, the promulgation of this Emergency Decree is deemed necessary.

Paragraph.text: Dr. Kullaya Tantitemit discussed Thailand’s progress in implementing measures to address taxation in the digital economy under the OECD/G20 Inclusive Framework’s Two-Pillar Solution. Under Pillar 2, the Revenue Department is currently preparing the legal framework in line with the Global Anti-Base Erosion (GloBE) Model Rules, and has also proposed the Subject to Tax Rule (STTR) within the Thai context to maximize the effectiveness of the country’s tax administration.

Paragraph.text: To support the new tax system (Global Minimum Tax) of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) and to ensure Thailand remains able to attract investment and maintain the production base of large multinational corporations.
Document.Title: Investment promotion measures to mitigiate the effect of the new tax system

Paragraph.text: Large companies are facing impacts from the GMT tax decree, which mandates a minimum corporate tax rate of 15% effective January 1, 2025. The BOI Secretary-General revealed that approximately 1,000 Thai and foreign companies are affected by this law. The BOI is preparing two temporary mitigation measures: extending tax payment deadlines and leveraging its competitiveness fund. They are also urgently scheduling a meeting with the Ministry of Finance to finalize a "tax credit" scheme to support businesses and attract investment. Meanwhile, the Revenue Department is rushing to draft 20-30 subsidiary laws to support corporate income tax calculations according to OECD guidelines. Large listed companies are experiencing a drop in their share prices; TU reported that it is currently assessing the impact.
Document.Title: GMT tax shakes 1,000 companies; Ministry of Finance and BOI rush to finalize relief measures.

Paragraph.text: Mr. Narut Teodsathirasak, Secretary-General of the Board of Investment (BOI), revealed to "Prachachat Business" that the BOI is preparing measures to mitigate the impact of joining the OECD, or the 15% GMT tariff increase on multinational corporations. This will definitely affect investment planning for approximately 1,000 companies, such as electronics component manufacturers and automotive manufacturers, among others.

Paragraph.text: In addition, the BOI has prepared two more measures: 1. Investment promotion measures to mitigate the impact of the new tax collection approach (BOI Announcement No. 1/2023 dated May 16, 2023), which allows companies receiving investment promotion to choose to receive a 50% reduction in corporate income tax from the normal rate, instead of the exemption from corporate income tax. This will be extended for twice the duration, but not exceeding 10 years, so that the effective tax rate (ETR) of each company each year is closer to 15%, resulting in lower additional tax payments for each company and longer benefit period.

Paragraph.text: Measures to enhance the competitiveness of large enterprises in the country's target industries. Paragraph.text: Mr. Lawaron Saengsanit, Permanent Secretary of the Ministry of Finance, stated that imposing a minimum corporate income tax of 15% is a global tax norm to attract investment. Tax authorities worldwide have agreed on this to prevent competitive tax cuts. As Thailand is a member of the OECD, it must enact legislation to implement this tax. He acknowledged that this will impact listed companies, but added that even if Thailand didn't impose this rate, other countries would still collect 15%.

Paragraph.text: Regarding relief for affected businesses, the Board of Investment (BOI) needs to reach an agreement with the Revenue Department again to establish guidelines for "Qualified Refundable Tax Credits" (QRTC). Previously, the draft law proposed that a portion of the increased tax revenue be allocated to a fund to enhance the competitiveness of future industries, to be used for private sector compensation. However, the Office of the Council of State indicated that such a measure might not be feasible due to potential violations of public financial discipline regulations, and therefore removed that provision.

Paragraph.text: "Instead of directly using collected tax revenue for compensation, a better approach would be to provide tax credits to targeted industries that meet the BOI's investment conditions. Paragraph.text: The Revenue Department stated that over the past decade, multinational corporations have attempted to allocate profits and diversify investments abroad, taking advantage of various investment attraction measures, including corporate income tax benefits. This has led to countries competing to reduce taxes for foreign companies investing in Thailand, resulting in increasingly lower effective tax rates. The OECD therefore proposed reforms to the international tax system to ensure fairer taxation of multinational corporations.
